clusters.title: Scientists link childhood stress to lifelong digestive issues
clusters.summary: Early life stress may set the stage for long-term digestive problems by disrupting the gut-brain connection. Studies in both mice and thousands of children found links to symptoms like pain, constipation, and IBS. Scientists discovered that different biological pathways control different gut issues, hinting at more personalized treatments in the future.

items.title: Scientists link childhood stress to lifelong digestive issues
items.description: Early life stress may set the stage for long-term digestive problems by disrupting the gut-brain connection. Studies in both mice and thousands of children found links to symptoms like pain, constipation, and IBS. Scientists discovered that different biological pathways control different gut issues, hinting at more personalized treatments in the future. The research also highlights how a child’s early environment can have lasting physical effects—not just emotional ones.

clusters.title: New clues to hepatitis B species restriction could help build a novel model for studying infection
clusters.summary: Some 254 million people live with a chronic hepatitis B (HBV) infection that is often asymptomatic for decades, only to emerge in an advanced stage of disease that turns to fatal cirrhosis or liver cancer in nearly a million people every year. The development of effective treatments for HBV infections has been stymied because we lack a good small animal model for studying the entire viral lifecycle, host response, and disease progression.

items.title: New clues to hepatitis B species restriction could help build a novel model for studying infection
items.description: Some 254 million people live with a chronic hepatitis B (HBV) infection that is often asymptomatic for decades, only to emerge in an advanced stage of disease that turns to fatal cirrhosis or liver cancer in nearly a million people every year. The development of effective treatments for HBV infections has been stymied because we lack a good small animal model for studying the entire viral lifecycle, host response, and disease progression.

clusters.title: This routine heart scan sees the danger coming long before symptoms strike
clusters.summary: A routine heart scan might soon do more than just check for clogged arteries; it could act as a crystal ball for your cardiac health. Researchers at Kumamoto University have revealed that by combining two specific markers from a standard cardiac Computed Tomography (CT) scan, they can identify patients at the highest risk for future heart failure and death.

items.title: This routine heart scan sees the danger coming long before symptoms strike

Researchers at Kumamoto University have revealed that by combining two specific markers from a standard cardiac Computed Tomography (CT) scan, they can identify patients at the highest risk for future heart failure and death.

clusters.title: Lifting weights builds a sharper mind and reduces anxiety in older women
clusters.summary: A recent clinical trial reveals that resistance training offers major cognitive and psychological benefits for older women. Regardless of whether participants lifted heavy or light weights, the exercise routine drastically improved memory scores and reduced depressive symptoms.

items.title: Lifting weights builds a sharper mind and reduces anxiety in older women
items.description: A recent clinical trial reveals that resistance training offers major cognitive and psychological benefits for older women. Regardless of whether participants lifted heavy or light weights, the exercise routine drastically improved memory scores and reduced depressive symptoms.

clusters.title: Scientists supercharge immune cells to destroy cancer more effectively
clusters.summary: Researchers have found a way to make cancer-killing immune cells more powerful and precise. By adding specific signaling components, they boosted the cells’ readiness to attack tumors. Surprisingly, briefly suppressing the cells with a drug before use made them even more effective later.

items.title: Scientists supercharge immune cells to destroy cancer more effectively
items.description: Researchers have found a way to make cancer-killing immune cells more powerful and precise. By adding specific signaling components, they boosted the cells’ readiness to attack tumors. Surprisingly, briefly suppressing the cells with a drug before use made them even more effective later. The approach could help create safer, stronger next-gen cancer treatments.

clusters.title: Study finds dangerous lead levels in children’s clothing
clusters.summary: Fast fashion might come with a hidden danger: lead. Researchers testing children’s shirts from multiple retailers found every sample exceeded U.S. safety limits, raising concerns about toxic exposure—especially since young kids often chew on clothing.

items.title: Study finds dangerous lead levels in children’s clothing
items.description: Fast fashion might come with a hidden danger: lead. Researchers testing children’s shirts from multiple retailers found every sample exceeded U.S. safety limits, raising concerns about toxic exposure—especially since young kids often chew on clothing. Brightly colored fabrics like red and yellow showed particularly high levels, likely due to chemicals used to fix dyes. Simulations suggest that even brief mouthing could expose children to unsafe amounts of lead, a substance known to harm brain development and behavior.

clusters.title: California Lawmakers Seek Protections for Patients in ICE Custody
clusters.summary: Moving through the California Senate are two bills, informed by KFF Health News reporting, that would strengthen protections for patients brought to health facilities by Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

items.title: California Lawmakers Seek Protections for Patients in ICE Custody
items.description: Moving through the California Senate are two bills, informed by KFF Health News reporting, that would strengthen protections for patients brought to health facilities by Immigration and Customs Enforcement.

clusters.title: How an anti-inflammatory drug helps the heart after a heart attack
clusters.summary: Every day, thousands of people worldwide suffer a heart attack, often leading to lasting damage to the heart muscle. Recent research from the University of Oslo suggests that targeting inflammation in the body helps protect the heart and improve recovery after a heart attack. The work is published in the journal eBioMedicine.

items.title: How an anti-inflammatory drug helps the heart after a heart attack
items.description: Every day, thousands of people worldwide suffer a heart attack, often leading to lasting damage to the heart muscle. Recent research from the University of Oslo suggests that targeting inflammation in the body helps protect the heart and improve recovery after a heart attack. The work is published in the journal eBioMedicine.

clusters.title: Scientists just watched Alzheimer’s damage happen in real time
clusters.summary: Scientists at Oregon State University have captured something researchers have long struggled to see: the real-time chemical interactions that help drive Alzheimer’s disease. By watching how metal ions—especially copper—trigger harmful protein clumping in the brain, the team uncovered a clearer picture of how the disease develops at a molecular level.

items.title: Scientists just watched Alzheimer’s damage happen in real time
items.description: Scientists at Oregon State University have captured something researchers have long struggled to see: the real-time chemical interactions that help drive Alzheimer’s disease. By watching how metal ions—especially copper—trigger harmful protein clumping in the brain, the team uncovered a clearer picture of how the disease develops at a molecular level.

clusters.title: ADHD brains show sleep-like activity even while awake
clusters.summary: Researchers have identified a surprising brain pattern that may help explain why people with ADHD often struggle to stay focused. Even while awake, their brains can slip into brief episodes of “sleep-like” activity during demanding tasks. These moments are linked to more mistakes, slower reaction times, and lapses in attention.

items.title: ADHD brains show sleep-like activity even while awake
items.description: Researchers have identified a surprising brain pattern that may help explain why people with ADHD often struggle to stay focused. Even while awake, their brains can slip into brief episodes of “sleep-like” activity during demanding tasks. These moments are linked to more mistakes, slower reaction times, and lapses in attention.

clusters.title: Scientists discover hidden brain switch that tells you to stop eating
clusters.summary: Your brain’s “stop eating” signal may come from an unexpected source. Researchers found that astrocytes—once thought to just support neurons—actually play a key role in controlling appetite. After a meal, glucose triggers tanycytes, which send signals to astrocytes that then activate fullness neurons.

items.title: Scientists discover hidden brain switch that tells you to stop eating
items.description: Your brain’s “stop eating” signal may come from an unexpected source. Researchers found that astrocytes—once thought to just support neurons—actually play a key role in controlling appetite. After a meal, glucose triggers tanycytes, which send signals to astrocytes that then activate fullness neurons. This newly discovered pathway could lead to innovative treatments for obesity and eating disorders.

clusters.title: Scientists just found a hidden “drain” inside the human brain
clusters.summary: A hidden waste-removal pathway in the brain has finally been caught in action. Using cutting-edge MRI scans, researchers discovered that fluid flows along the middle meningeal artery in a slow, lymphatic-like pattern—very different from blood. This confirms the presence of a previously unknown drainage hub in humans.

items.title: Scientists just found a hidden “drain” inside the human brain
items.description: A hidden waste-removal pathway in the brain has finally been caught in action. Using cutting-edge MRI scans, researchers discovered that fluid flows along the middle meningeal artery in a slow, lymphatic-like pattern—very different from blood. This confirms the presence of a previously unknown drainage hub in humans. The finding could transform how scientists approach brain aging, injury, and diseases like Alzheimer’s.

clusters.title: AI identifies early risk patterns for skin cancer
clusters.summary: A massive Swedish study shows that AI can spot people at higher risk of melanoma using routine health data. Advanced models significantly outperformed basic methods, identifying high-risk groups with striking accuracy. Some individuals flagged by the system had up to a 33% chance of developing melanoma within five years.

items.title: AI identifies early risk patterns for skin cancer
items.description: A massive Swedish study shows that AI can spot people at higher risk of melanoma using routine health data. Advanced models significantly outperformed basic methods, identifying high-risk groups with striking accuracy. Some individuals flagged by the system had up to a 33% chance of developing melanoma within five years. This approach could pave the way for smarter, more targeted screening.

clusters.title: New psychology research shows people consistently underestimate how often things go wrong across society
clusters.summary: A new study reveals a widespread "failure gap," where people systematically underestimate how often bad outcomes happen. This bias is linked to how rarely failures are discussed in the news and on social media.

items.title: New psychology research shows people consistently underestimate how often things go wrong across society
items.description: A new study reveals a widespread "failure gap," where people systematically underestimate how often bad outcomes happen. This bias is linked to how rarely failures are discussed in the news and on social media.

clusters.title: Study reveals that 13,000 patients waited over three days in England's emergency departments last year
clusters.summary: Exclusive data published in The BMJ today show that 13,386 patients in England waited at least three days for A&E (accident and emergency) treatment last year, part of almost 500,000 who spent over 24 hours stuck in NHS emergency departments—before either being admitted, transferred, or discharged—in 2025.

items.title: Study reveals that 13,000 patients waited over three days in England's emergency departments last year
items.description: Exclusive data published in The BMJ today show that 13,386 patients in England waited at least three days for A&E (accident and emergency) treatment last year, part of almost 500,000 who spent over 24 hours stuck in NHS emergency departments—before either being admitted, transferred, or discharged—in 2025.

clusters.title: Two common drugs may reverse fatty liver disease, study finds
clusters.summary: Scientists have discovered that combining two existing drugs can dramatically reduce liver fat linked to a common and often silent disease. The treatment not only improved liver health in animal models but also showed potential to lower heart-related risks. Interestingly, using lower doses of both drugs together worked just as well as higher doses alone.

items.title: Two common drugs may reverse fatty liver disease, study finds
items.description: Scientists have discovered that combining two existing drugs can dramatically reduce liver fat linked to a common and often silent disease. The treatment not only improved liver health in animal models but also showed potential to lower heart-related risks. Interestingly, using lower doses of both drugs together worked just as well as higher doses alone. While promising, the findings still need to be tested in humans.

clusters.title: Scientists finally crack mystery of rare COVID vaccine blood clots
clusters.summary: Researchers have uncovered why a rare blood clotting disorder can occur after certain COVID-19 vaccines or adenovirus infections. The immune system can mistakenly target a normal blood protein (PF4) after confusing it with a viral protein. This triggers clotting in extremely rare cases.

items.title: Scientists finally crack mystery of rare COVID vaccine blood clots
items.description: Researchers have uncovered why a rare blood clotting disorder can occur after certain COVID-19 vaccines or adenovirus infections. The immune system can mistakenly target a normal blood protein (PF4) after confusing it with a viral protein. This triggers clotting in extremely rare cases. The breakthrough means vaccines can now be redesigned to avoid this reaction while staying effective.

clusters.title: Can doctors discuss a president's mental health? Why ethics and public risk collide
clusters.summary: As the oldest person elected president of the United States, Donald Trump has long faced questions about his health. But is it appropriate for doctors to comment publicly on a president's mental health? In an opinion article published in The BMJ, David Nicholl and Trisha Greenhalgh examine the ethical tensions involved.

items.title: Can doctors discuss a president's mental health? Why ethics and public risk collide
items.description: As the oldest person elected president of the United States, Donald Trump has long faced questions about his health. But is it appropriate for doctors to comment publicly on a president's mental health? In an opinion article published in The BMJ, David Nicholl and Trisha Greenhalgh examine the ethical tensions involved. While heads of state are entitled to medical confidentiality, their decisions can have far-reaching consequences, raising the question of whether professional norms against public commentary should ever be set aside.

clusters.title: Can sparkling water boost metabolism and help with weight loss?
clusters.summary: Sparkling water is often seen as a simple, healthy drink—but could it also help with weight loss? New research suggests it may slightly boost how the body processes blood sugar and energy. However, the effect is very small, meaning it’s no substitute for diet and exercise.

items.title: Can sparkling water boost metabolism and help with weight loss?
items.description: Sparkling water is often seen as a simple, healthy drink—but could it also help with weight loss? New research suggests it may slightly boost how the body processes blood sugar and energy. However, the effect is very small, meaning it’s no substitute for diet and exercise.

clusters.title: The people you live with could be changing your gut bacteria
clusters.summary: Spending time with close companions might do more than strengthen bonds—it could also reshape your gut bacteria. In a study of island birds, those with stronger social ties shared more gut microbes, especially types that require direct contact to spread. This suggests that social interaction itself—not just shared space—drives microbial exchange.

items.title: The people you live with could be changing your gut bacteria
items.description: Spending time with close companions might do more than strengthen bonds—it could also reshape your gut bacteria. In a study of island birds, those with stronger social ties shared more gut microbes, especially types that require direct contact to spread. This suggests that social interaction itself—not just shared space—drives microbial exchange. The same process may be happening in human households through everyday closeness.
